header.title: Daily FI Tape
header.as_of_note: NY close briefing; Asia update is shown separately.
header.latest_update_cutoff: 2026-06-08 close plus the next Taipei morning update

bullets: UST2Y fell 2 bp while UST10Y and UST30Y rose, steepening 2s10s to about 41 bp and keeping real-yield pressure alive.
Technology equities rebounded and VIX fell, but crude rose and ECB/global duration risk kept the session from becoming a clean risk-on reversal.
Credit remained borrower-specific, with AI infrastructure access set against FX refinancing and private-credit transparency concerns.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: The front end stabilized after the payroll shock, but long-end yields stayed firm as oil risk, Fed pricing, Japanese duration demand, and selective credit conditions kept the tape unresolved.

top_drivers.title: Long-end resistance replaced front-end panic
top_drivers.short_summary: The curve steepened as the market stopped extending Friday’s front-end selloff but still demanded compensation for inflation and policy uncertainty.
top_drivers.detail: UST2Y fell 2 bp, UST10Y rose 1 bp, UST30Y rose 2 bp, and TIPS10Y rose 2 bp, leaving the close less dovish than the equity rebound suggested.

top_drivers.title: Oil and global central banks kept inflation risk alive
top_drivers.short_summary: Middle East energy risk and ECB credibility concerns made the rates backdrop global rather than purely U.S. payroll-driven.
top_drivers.detail: WTI rose 0.84%, Brent rose 1.25%, and the reading set carried scenarios in which sustained oil stress would pressure LNG, agriculture, and central-bank reaction functions.

top_drivers.title: Credit was open with FX and opacity risk
top_drivers.short_summary: AI-linked borrowers still had funding access, but FX maturities and private-credit rating quality kept credit selection tight.
top_drivers.detail: CoreWeave and AI financing showed capital availability, while Philippine corporate maturities and private-credit loss analysis pointed to hidden balance-sheet sensitivity.

latest_asia_premarket.summary: Separately, Asia/pre-market pricing before the Taipei morning update shows 10Y Treasury note futures 108.94 (-0.33%); 30Y Treasury bond futures 111.28 (-0.64%); S&P 500 futures 7,401 (-0.2%); Nasdaq 100 futures 29,382 (-0.25%); WTI crude futures 91.43 (+0.14%).

items.title: 10Y Treasury note futures
items.detail: 108.94, -0.33% from the prior close. Treasury futures give the cleanest early read on whether the duration bid held after the NY close

items.title: 30Y Treasury bond futures
items.detail: 111.28, -0.64% from the prior close. Long-bond futures help separate a persistent long-end bid from a one-session cash-market move

items.title: S&P 500 futures
items.detail: 7,401, -0.2% from the prior close. Equity futures show whether the overnight update confirmed or faded the NY risk tone

items.title: Nasdaq 100 futures
items.detail: 29,382, -0.25% from the prior close. Nasdaq futures are the early check on whether growth and technology beta stabilized

items.title: WTI crude futures
items.detail: 91.43, +0.14% from the prior close. WTI futures test whether the oil channel was still easing inflation pressure by the Taipei morning update

watch_events.title: Fed hike pricing after payrolls
watch_events.why_it_matters: A December hike remained in market pricing even after the front end stabilized.

watch_events.title: Japanese foreign-bond demand
watch_events.why_it_matters: Record May foreign-bond purchases could support U.S. duration, but only if Fed and Middle East uncertainty calmed.

watch_events.title: FX refinancing risk
watch_events.why_it_matters: Large offshore maturities in Asia kept hard-currency credit sensitive to dollar and local-currency moves.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: WTI rose 0.84% on the day and was -0.93% over one week; Brent rose 1.25%. Energy prices rose, but 10Y breakevens fell 1 bp, so inflation compensation did not follow crude higher.

cross_asset_cards.title: Equities
cross_asset_cards.summary: SPX was +0.3%, NDX +1.58%, and SOX +5.61%; the equity tape was risk-on, technology beta led, while VIX fell 12%.

cross_asset_cards.title: Credit
cross_asset_cards.summary: LQD was -0.1%, HYG +0.14%, JNK +0.07%, and EMB -0.13%; these are ETF total returns, not direct OAS or CDX. Treasury duration was roughly neutral and ETF returns were mixed, so credit confirmation remained incomplete.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: DXY was little changed while gold was little changed; Treasury yields were little changed, leaving macro confirmation incomplete.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening fixed-income thesis

June 8 was not a clean reversal of the June 5 rates shock. It was a more uneven session in which the front end stopped leading the selloff, the long end stayed heavy, technology equities rebounded, and global inflation risk remained alive through oil and non-U.S. central-bank pressure. UST2Y finished near 4.15%, down 2 bp, while UST5Y was essentially unchanged near 4.29%. UST10Y rose 1 bp to around 4.56%, and UST30Y rose 2 bp to about 5.03%. The curve steepened: 2s10s moved to roughly 41 bp, 3 bp steeper, and 2s30s reached about 88 bp, 4 bp steeper. That shape matters. It says the market was no longer extending Friday’s front-end shock in a straight line, but it was still unwilling to buy duration aggressively while oil, Fed pricing, and global-rate pressure remained unresolved.

The day’s market data produced a split tape. Equities recovered, with the S&P 500 up 0.30%, Nasdaq 100 up 1.58%, and SOX up 5.61%. VIX fell about 12%. WTI rose 0.84% and Brent rose 1.25%, keeping the energy-inflation channel active. The dollar was almost flat, gold was little changed, and credit instruments were mixed: LQD fell 0.10%, HYG rose 0.14%, JNK rose 0.07%, and EMB fell 0.13%. That is not a broad risk-on confirmation. It is a market stabilizing after a sharp growth-stock drawdown while still carrying higher-rate and commodity-inflation risk.

The day's reporting gave the fixed-income driver a global frame. Treasuries had already been under pressure as investors reassessed the strong U.S. jobs report and Middle East escalation. Hike pricing remained relevant, with traders again assigning weight to a quarter-point Fed increase by December and some probability to a second move. At the same time, Japanese pension-fund flow estimates showed record foreign-bond purchases in May, encouraged by higher U.S. yields and a roughly 200 bp gap between U.S. and Japanese long yields. That overseas demand is supportive for U.S. duration when uncertainty clears, but the conditionality is the point: Japanese buying was tied to clarity on the Middle East and Fed policy, not to a blind reach for yield.

The most important macro risk was oil-led inflation. The day’s crude move was not extreme, but the reading set carried scenarios in which oil could rise toward $120 for a sustained period, raising LNG, refined-product, agriculture, and fertilizer costs. That risk fed directly into the ECB discussion, where some economists warned that a credibility-driven hike response could repeat the policy error of 2011. The U.S. close therefore had to be read alongside Europe and Asia. The bond market was dealing with a shared question: whether central banks were still fighting the last inflation impulse or being forced into a new one.

Credit conditions were stable only at the surface. Higher-quality risk assets recovered, but the underlying fixed-income stories were about refinancing, FX exposure, private-credit opacity, and AI funding scale. CoreWeave’s continuing bond-market activity and Nvidia-linked expansion showed that AI infrastructure financing remained open. Philippine conglomerates facing about $26 billion of debt maturities showed the other side of the same market: borrowers could meet refinancing needs through bonds, bank funding, and internal liquidity, but FX and leverage risk required monitoring. The credit read was therefore selective and conditional, not broadly easy.

## Rates, curve, and Fed pricing

The Treasury close carried a different message from June 5. UST2Y moved down 2 bp, while UST10Y and UST30Y rose 1 bp and 2 bp. That bear-steepening pattern is less about immediate front-end panic and more about the market asking whether long-end compensation is sufficient while inflation risks persist. The 2s10s steepening to about 41 bp and 2s30s to about 88 bp showed that the curve was giving back some of the prior front-end-led flattening. But the weekly and monthly context still looked hawkish: UST2Y was up 10 bp on the week and 25 bp on the month, UST5Y up 11 bp on the week and 27 bp on the month, and UST10Y up 9 bp on the week and 18 bp on the month.

Real yields continued to matter. TIPS10Y ended near 2.21%, up 2 bp on the day, while 10Y breakevens slipped 1 bp to about 235 bp. That combination is not a pure inflation-compensation selloff. It is a market demanding higher real yield even as measured breakevens edged lower. The real-yield pressure fits the Fed-pricing backdrop: if the labor market allows the Fed to defend tighter policy against energy inflation, nominal yields can stay high through real rates rather than through wider breakevens.

The reading set’s Fed facts were explicit. Treasuries had fallen as investors reassessed a strong jobs report, and traders had returned to pricing a quarter-point Fed hike by December, with a smaller probability of a second increase. The logic was not that every data point demanded a hike immediately. It was that labor resilience made it easier for the central bank to justify keeping policy tight or moving tighter if energy and inflation pressure persisted. That is why the rates market did not simply rally after Friday’s shock. The front end stabilized, but the policy-risk premium did not disappear.

Japan added a second layer to the rates story. Japanese pension-fund flow estimates showed record foreign-bond purchases in May after U.S. yields rose, and the gap between Japanese and U.S. long yields remained wide. That buying can help absorb U.S. duration, but the reading set framed it as conditional on Middle East and Fed clarity. In other words, foreign demand exists, but it is not a guarantee against higher U.S. yields. If oil volatility or Fed uncertainty rises, overseas duration demand can pause even when yield levels look attractive.

The curve therefore had two messages. First, the front-end shock was not worsening on June 8. Second, long-end duration was not yet comfortable. A 5s30s-style long-end debate was not the whole story, but the day’s 30Y move and steepening showed that investors still wanted compensation for macro uncertainty. The result was a market stuck between buying the post-payroll front-end dip and respecting the broader inflation and oil risks that kept real yields elevated.

Indonesia added a more direct market-access angle. Its sovereign wealth fund Danantara was preparing a U.S. dollar bond roadshow while policymakers were trying to stabilize the currency and attract inflows. That matters because it places a new borrower in front of global investors at the same moment the country’s assets were under pressure. If the roadshow can clear without a large concession, it would show that strategic issuance still has access despite macro stress. If investors demand a heavy premium, the episode becomes a live test of how quickly currency pressure feeds into hard-currency funding costs.

## Supply, term premium, and liquidity

The direct Treasury supply evidence for June 8 was limited, so the useful read focuses on actual financing and liquidity facts rather than inferred auction demand from the yield curve. The supported evidence pointed to corporate refinancing, FX risk, and market access rather than to a broad Treasury-market functioning claim. Philippine conglomerates were described as facing about $26 billion of debt maturities, and the relevant credit risk was not that they had no funding channels. It was that refinancing through bond issuance, bank funding, and internal liquidity still left FX-related vulnerabilities visible.

That distinction matters for term premium. Long-end U.S. yields were heavy, but the day did not provide direct evidence that Treasury supply itself had become harder to absorb. What it did show was that the global fixed-income market was requiring borrowers to think harder about the currency and duration mix of their liabilities. When the dollar is firm, U.S. rates are high, and oil risk can raise import bills, offshore borrowers face a more complicated refinancing decision. The liquidity issue is less about one auction and more about how balance sheets behave when multiple funding channels are available but none are cheap.

AI infrastructure funding was another term-premium-adjacent theme, but it belongs mostly in credit. CoreWeave’s continued bond-market activity and deals tied to Nvidia showed that capital-intensive AI infrastructure still had access to debt. China’s Moonshot AI seeking a very high valuation in new funding talks showed that capital demand in the AI ecosystem remained enormous. Those facts do not prove aggregate crowding in the Treasury market. They do, however, explain why investors cared about credit discipline even on a day when technology equities rebounded. A market can finance AI buildout and still worry about future refinancing supply.

The supply conclusion is therefore modest. Long-end rates were firm, and overseas investors were yield-sensitive, but the hard evidence was about borrower-level funding and currency risk. The correct market implication is that liquidity was available with conditions. It was available to borrowers with strategic relevance or adequate structure. It was less forgiving for balance sheets exposed to FX depreciation, private-credit opacity, or energy-cost shocks. That is enough to affect credit and term-premium thinking without overstating what the yield curve alone can prove.

Korea showed how an AI growth story can be negative for domestic bonds even when it supports equities. Investor enthusiasm around semiconductors and AI had lifted stocks but pushed government bonds into one of the weakest performances globally, with three-year yields near the highest since 2023. That is important for credit because it separates sector momentum from funding conditions. The same AI cycle that lifts equity valuations can raise inflation expectations, central-bank pressure, and local yields, making debt funding less forgiving.

The private-credit rating issue made that concern more structural. Insurers and other long-duration investors were accumulating private-credit exposure, while the reading set pointed to evidence that privately rated assets had higher loss incidence than public bonds assigned similar grades. That does not say private credit was failing on June 8. It says that a higher-rate, oil-sensitive backdrop makes opaque credit risk more dangerous. If investors cannot see losses quickly, refinancing events become the moment when the hidden spread risk appears.

## Credit conditions

Credit conditions on June 8 were better than the prior equity panic, but still selective. The credit ETF tape was mixed: HYG and JNK rose slightly, LQD declined a little, and EMB weakened. Because those prices include duration and rates effects, they should not be read as clean spread measures. The more useful evidence came from borrower stories. The market was still open to AI infrastructure and private-credit-linked borrowers, but it was also flagging FX, leverage, and rating-transparency risks.

CoreWeave was a central example. The company had been raising money in the bond market alongside its IPO path and continued signing Nvidia-related deals. That indicates lender appetite for AI infrastructure had not vanished after the prior technology selloff. But the credit question is not only whether funding exists; it is whether financing terms still make sense if equity valuations and future cash-flow assumptions become more volatile. The day’s SOX rebound helped risk sentiment, but it did not remove the structural need for repeated capital raises across AI infrastructure.

The Philippine maturity wall gave the credit section a different tone. Large conglomerates facing about $26 billion of maturities had met refinancing needs through a mix of bond issuance, bank funding, and internal liquidity, yet FX-related risk remained a concern. That is a classic hard-currency credit issue. If local-currency earnings are used to service foreign-currency liabilities, a stronger dollar or weaker domestic currency can change debt-service capacity even when funding channels remain open. It also shows why the credit read cannot be reduced to U.S. high-yield ETFs.

Private-credit risk also appeared in the reading set. Insurers and carriers were described as increasing private-credit exposure, while analysis of privately rated assets suggested higher credit-loss incidence than similarly rated public debt. That does not mean the private-credit market was failing on June 8. It means that investors should treat the asset class as a source of hidden beta when macro volatility rises. Private ratings, illiquidity, and slower price discovery can delay recognition of stress until refinancing events force it into the open.

The day’s credit conclusion is selective stability. Risk appetite improved enough for technology shares and high-yield credit to rebound, but the underlying credit stories were still about balance-sheet resilience, FX liabilities, and the durability of AI cash flows. That is consistent with a fixed-income market that is not closed, but also not easy. Higher real rates, oil risk, and central-bank uncertainty kept the marginal credit question focused on borrower quality.

Indonesia and Korea also broadened the global-duration read. Indonesia’s officials were trying to stabilize markets and defend inflows before a dollar-bond roadshow, while Korea’s AI-led domestic growth impulse had pushed government yields higher. These are not identical stories, but both show that global duration was being pulled by local growth, currency, and policy pressures rather than simply following the U.S. curve. The U.S. long end therefore could not rally only because the front end paused; it had to compete with a global set of markets where higher nominal growth or currency defense was lifting local yields.

The geopolitical tail was not abstract. The reading set’s Taiwan scenario estimated a very large hit to Europe in a worst-case conflict, and the Middle East oil scenario mapped into LNG, agriculture, and fertilizer costs. Those scenarios were not NY-close price drivers in the same way as Treasury yields or crude futures, but they explain why volatility premia did not vanish when equities bounced. They keep investors from treating a single-day technology rebound as an all-clear signal for global duration and EM credit.

## Global duration, EM hard-currency credit, and cross-market confirmation

Global duration was central to June 8. Japan was important because domestic investors had shown willingness to buy foreign bonds when U.S. yields were high enough. The record foreign-bond purchases attributed to Japanese pension-fund accounts in May suggest that yield differentials can pull overseas money toward U.S. duration. But that support was explicitly conditional on clarity around Fed policy and the Middle East. In fixed-income terms, foreign demand was a stabilizer, not a free put option.

Europe added an inflation-credibility dilemma. The ECB discussion was framed around the risk of repeating a 2011-style mistake: tightening into an energy shock to protect credibility, then discovering that growth had already weakened. That risk mattered for U.S. rates because global duration is jointly priced when energy inflation is the common shock. If European central banks tighten because oil, LNG, and refined-product costs rise, U.S. long-end investors cannot treat energy as a local story. Oil at $91 WTI and $94 Brent on June 8 was not a crisis close, but the scenario analysis around a much larger shock kept inflation tails alive.

EM hard-currency credit was also under pressure through country-specific channels. Indonesia-related stress and FX defense were part of the regional backdrop. Philippine corporate maturities added a private-sector refinancing layer. The broader Taiwan-conflict scenario, with estimates of severe damage to Europe and supply-chain exposure, was not a day-one market driver, but it explained why geopolitical risk could enter duration and credit premia even without an immediate U.S. data surprise. Those risks were tail risks, but fixed-income markets price tail risks through currency, commodity, and term-premium channels.

Cross-asset confirmation was mixed. Equities rebounded and VIX fell, which argued against an accelerating risk-off event. Oil rose, which argued against declaring inflation pressure finished. The dollar was flat, gold was flat, and credit was mixed. That combination says the market stabilized after Friday but did not invalidate the hawkish rates regime. If the day had been a full risk-on reversal, long-end yields might have rallied or credit would have shown broader strength. Instead, the tape pointed to recovery in beaten-down growth assets while fixed-income investors kept demanding compensation for policy and commodity uncertainty.

The global conclusion is that June 8 sat between relief and resistance. Japan could supply duration demand, but not unconditionally. Europe faced the credibility-versus-growth problem. EM borrowers still carried FX and refinancing risk. Oil kept the inflation tail open. The U.S. curve therefore steepened not because the Fed story was gone, but because the market was redistributing the risk from front-end panic into long-end and global uncertainty.

The fifth watchpoint was whether AI-linked financing remains a credit stabilizer or becomes a duration headwind. CoreWeave-style financing, Moonshot valuation discussions, and Korean AI-driven yield pressure point in different directions. If AI cash-flow expectations stay credible, lenders can keep funding infrastructure buildout. If the theme lifts local inflation and bond yields while equity valuations become more volatile, the same capital cycle can become a source of refinancing and duration stress.

A sixth watchpoint was whether Korea-style AI strength keeps lifting local yields. The Korea story showed that a technology growth impulse can hurt bonds when investors read it as stronger domestic demand and higher inflation pressure. That is relevant beyond Korea because AI investment is becoming a funding and macro variable, not just an equity theme. If semiconductor demand supports growth but raises local rate expectations, global duration markets may face resistance even on days when U.S. front-end yields stabilize.

A seventh watchpoint was Danantara’s U.S. roadshow. It was a clean test of whether investors would separate a strategic dollar issuer from the pressure on Indonesia’s currency and local bonds. Strong demand would argue that hard-currency access remained open; weak demand would confirm that local stress was spilling into offshore funding.

## Market implications and watchpoints

The first watchpoint was Fed pricing. The market had stepped back from Friday’s most aggressive front-end impulse, but December hike pricing remained in the frame. If incoming inflation data confirmed sticky prices, the front end could regain leadership. If the Fed pushed back against hike expectations, the long end would still need to decide whether oil and global duration risks justify a higher real-yield term structure.

The second watchpoint was Japanese demand for foreign bonds. The May purchase data showed that higher U.S. yields can attract overseas buyers, but that demand depends on policy and geopolitical clarity. A renewed Middle East escalation or a more hawkish Fed path could reduce the willingness to extend duration, even if nominal yields look attractive. Conversely, credible de-escalation and a stable Fed message could turn foreign demand into a stronger support for U.S. duration.

The third watchpoint was the credit divide. CoreWeave and AI-related borrowers showed that growth-linked financing channels remained open, but Philippine maturities, FX risk, and private-credit rating concerns showed where the fragility sits. If high-yield and private-credit borrowers can refinance without heavy concessions, the risk market can stabilize. If borrowers with FX exposure or opaque ratings start paying up materially, the credit market will confirm that the higher-rate regime is biting.

The fourth watchpoint was oil and Europe. A modest daily oil gain is manageable; an oil shock that forces the ECB and other central banks to defend credibility is not. The market needs to know whether the energy channel is a temporary premium or a renewed inflation impulse. June 8 did not answer that question. It showed that risk assets could recover while fixed income kept the question open.

methodology_note: Synthesized market analysis from market levels and reporting context. Asia and pre-market developments are separated from the close narrative.
